coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation of the effect of charging and discharging on a battery module of cylindrical lithium-ion cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Bhakre, Someshwar
Dhoble, Ashwinkumar
Kotambkar, Mangesh
coredata.dc:description: The generation of heat in Li-ion batteries (LIBs) plays a crucial role in determining their performance, safety, and longevity, particularly when subjected to different charging and discharging rates. This study conducts an experimental analysis of heat production in cylindrical Li-ion cells at different C-rates: 0.5 C, 0.75 C, and 1 C, during both charging and discharging phases. Through experiments involving constant current (CC) – constant voltage charging and constant current discharging, the thermal and electrical properties of the cells are examined. The findings indicate that higher C-rates result in greater heat production, with charging producing more heat than discharging. At a 1 C rate, the temperature of the battery cell increases to about 45°C during charging and 35°C during discharging. It is also observed that the most heat is generated during the constant current charging process, while less heat is produced during constant voltage charging. These results highlight the importance of thermal management strategies, particularly at higher C-rates, to mitigate excessive temperature rises and enhance battery reliability. This study contributes to improving the design of battery thermal management systems, ensuring the safe and efficient operation of LIBs in electric vehicles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cylindrical cell
Battery module
Charging
Discharging
Heat generation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermal performance of battery module during discharging and charging process
Thermal management techniques for high C-rates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Actual photograph of the experimental setup.
Variation of battery module cell temperature with time during discharge; a) at 0.5 C, b) at 0.75 C and c) 1 C rates.
Variation of battery module cell temperature with time during charge; a) at 0.5 C, b) at 0.75 C and c) 1 C rates.
Variation of current and voltage with time during charging at different C rates.
Variation of voltage with time during discharging at different C rate.
Variation of average temperature for battery module at different C- rate.
Variation of temperature and voltage at different C rates during discharge.
Variation of battery module maximum temperature with C rates.
Technical specifications of LG INR21700 M50T Lithium ion cell [27]

row.entry:
Maximum charge: 0°C to 45°C
Maximum discharge: −20°C to 60°C

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Average surface temperature of the lithium ion battery cells under different charge and discharge condition

row.entry: Average surface temperature of cell during discharging process (⁰C)
Average surface temperature of cell during charging process (⁰C)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermal management strategies for battery module

row.entry: forced-air pack design; Saechan (air + spray) experimental work.
Reported reductions vary: 3–10°C at moderate airflow; larger reductions with higher flow (depends on geometry).
Baseline vs airflow 1 m/s & 2 m/s at 0.75 C & 1 C; measure Tmax, ΔT, cooling power consumption.
PCM reviews and recent PCM-BTMS experiments (melting near 38–42°C).
Reported Tmax reductions 4–8°C for suitable PCM mass and placement; delays peak.
Baseline vs PCM sheet (melting 38–42°C) at 1 C charge: record Tmax, time-to-peak, PCM melt/solid cycle behavior.
Cell spacing effect studies and optimal pitch analyzes.
Improves uniformity; Tmax change modest unless combined with cooling — ΔT reduction of a few °C reported.
Baseline vs +1–2 mm spacing & aluminum spacers at 1 C: record Tmax and ΔT.
Hybrid (PCM + forced air / fins / conductive enhancement)
Multiple recent BTMS works show hybrid gives best performance.
Combined effects often exceeding individual: >8°C reduction possible; improved uniformity.
Test hybrid PCM + 1 m/s airflow at 1 C; compare to single methods.

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation of the effect of charging and discharging on a battery module of cylindrical lithium-ion cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Visvesvaraya National Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The generation of heat in Li-ion batteries (LIBs) plays a crucial role in determining their performance, safety, and longevity, particularly when subjected to different charging and discharging rates. This study conducts an experimental analysis of heat production in cylindrical Li-ion cells at different C-rates: 0.5 C, 0.75 C, and 1 C, during both charging and discharging phases. Through experiments involving constant current (CC) – constant voltage charging and constant current discharging, the thermal and electrical properties of the cells are examined. The findings indicate that higher C-rates result in greater heat production, with charging producing more heat than discharging. At a 1 C rate, the temperature of the battery cell increases to about 45°C during charging and 35°C during discharging. It is also observed that the most heat is generated during the constant current charging process, while less heat is produced during constant voltage charging. These results highlight the importance of thermal management strategies, particularly at higher C-rates, to mitigate excessive temperature rises and enhance battery reliability. This study contributes to improving the design of battery thermal management systems, ensuring the safe and efficient operation of LIBs in electric vehicles.

ce:section.ce:para: Air pollution is a significant issue that has led to climate change in the contemporary world. The emission of the greenhouse gases has caused a global warming, and therefore the average temperature of the earth has increased [1]. To deal with this challenge, the international shift to cleaner energy sources has revealed electric vehicles (EVs) as a potentially beneficial solution to building a cleaner and more sustainable future. This can largely be credited to the fact that the batteries have efficient energy storage and delivery capabilities and therefore, can be integrated with renewable energy systems [2].

Li-ion batteries (LIBs) are very popular because of their benefits such as having high power and energy density, long life, as well as being environmentally compatible. Such properties render them the best energy sources of EVs and also they can be used broadly in other electronic gadgets [3,4]. With the world transitioning to the scenario where traditional fuel powered vehicles are being substituted with new ones that are environmentally friendly, LIBs due to their high power (power output), energy density, and voltage performance have become a significant technology in the advancement of new energy vehicles [5]. In real-world applications, the performance demands of LIBs are quite different based on operating conditions like load requirements, temperature, and usage patterns. Among the most important factors affecting them, charging and discharging rates are crucial in defining the electrochemical behavior, efficiency, and thermal response of LIBs.

Battery operation includes transforming chemical energy into electrical energy at discharge and retaining it at charging. The both operations significantly impact battery performance. The temperature rise and overall performance are greatly influenced by discharge and charge rates, which attract significant research attention. Since temperature is a prominent marker of battery safety, knowing its response under different C-rates is of utmost importance. Onda et al. [6] tested the thermal response of cylindrical LiCoO₂ batteries subjected to high-rate charge-discharge cycles and observed a practically flat radial temperature distribution. Thus, highlighting the relevance of thermal analysis for secure EV battery operation. Panchal et al. [7] investigated the temperature profile of LiFePO₄/LFP battery packs over discharge rates from 1C-4C. Both peak and average temperature profiles were analyzed and result shows that peak discharge rates lead to a significant rise in average and peak surface temperatures. These observations highlight the thermal difficulties of high-rate discharging in battery packs. The investigation performed by Pra et al. [8] reported that the ideal battery pack operating depends on the cell chemistry, desired performance, and lifespan goals. Their study established a recommended temperature range for LIBs, and experimental outcomes at different discharge rates closely agreed with the predicted values. Huang et al. [9] derived a 1-D electrochemical-thermal coupling model was proposed for LiFePO₄ batteries to model the discharge process at discharge rates between 0.5 C and 4 C. The simulation results indicated that both the rate of electrochemical reaction and heat generation changed over time and at different locations within the battery. This non-uniform behavior increased with increasing discharge rate. The discharge capacity of LIBs decreases as temperature drops, regardless of the cathode material used. The lower temperatures slow down the electrochemical reactions, reducing the battery's discharge efficiency [10–13]. The above studies provide valuable insights for developing an effective and accurate thermal management for LIBs.

Capacity loss with time is one of the critical parameters of the electric vehicle and determines the battery life a priori. Wang et al. experimented on LiFePO4 batteries under discharge currents of 0.5 C - 5 C and the effect of various discharge rates on capacity that can be used. They proposed a total empirical degradation model which could be applied to determine the remaining useful life (RUL) in under different conditions of discharge [14]. Similarly, Yang et al. [15] also examined capacity fade within the same range of discharge rates and gave a dynamic Peukerts law, to aid in giving a corresponding capacity at other rates. A study of discharge tests on NMC batteries with different capacity levels conducted by Carlos et al. [16] revealed that actual capacities were often not the same as those indicated by the manufactures. They developed a fresh mathematical forecasting procedure of battery capacity at any discharge rate. A different study conducted by Ozdemir et al. [17] indicated that heat generation is highly influenced by the structure, nature and working conditions of a battery. The poor cooling through convection and radiation is what causes the rise in internal temperatures, which gradually affect the well-being and ability of the battery. Battery capacity degradation is one of the aspects which are necessary in order to accurately determine its RUL.

Two factors that have the most significant influence on the performance of LIBs are the internal resistance and temperature. The higher the internal resistance the more the energy lost and the better the heat generated, which reduces efficiency and accelerates degradation. Low temperatures increase resistance and reduce capacity but high temperatures improve short-term performance, although it can cause long-term damage. Both should therefore be adequately controlled to achieve battery health, safety and life. The battery internal resistance can be measured by several approaches and depends on various factors, such as the state of charge (SOC), temperature, and discharge rate. Together, high internal resistance and extreme temperatures undermine battery efficiency, restrict usable energy, and decrease the overall RUL and State of Health (SOH) of the battery [18,19]. The charge and discharge of battery pack is examined by Monem et al. [20] pointed out that two general charging methods, constant current (CC) and constant current–constant voltage are commonly used. Although the CC–CV method can achieve nearly full battery capacity, it requires more time compared to the CC method.

Chen et al. [21] developed a dynamic resistance model that incorporates these three factors; SOC, temperature, and discharge rate to estimate the internal resistance. Their findings indicated that, at the temperature of 25°C and discharge rates varying from 0.5 C to 3 C, the battery internal resistance fluctuated by as much as 10 milliohms. Liao et al. [22] examined thermally loaded LiNiCoMnO2 graphite batteries with different levels of SOC. The result shows that the highest temperature of the batteries increased linearly with the rise in SOC. Conversely, the onset temperature for thermal runaway lowered linearly with increased SOC levels. Lv et al. [23] examined the temperature dependence of capacity in LIBs with various anode materials. Their results indicate that the discharge capacity of the batteries is a cubic polynomial function of temperature, and the best operating conditions are between 20 and 50°C. The research also indicates that the internal resistance of batteries increases with time, especially when SOC is 0% or 100%, with SOC playing a major contribution to the polarization resistance. Santos et al. [24] tested the performance of lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) batteries under a temperature range (0–50°C) and different states of health (SOH). The findings showed that the battery has a much lesser capacity for storing and delivering charge at lower temperatures, especially around 0°C. The research also brought into focus that higher SOH batteries mainly experience charging through the CC mode, while lower SOH batteries depend more on the constant voltage (CV) mode. These findings indicate that temperature and SOH significantly influence the stored energy at various phases of the charging process. Wang et al. [25] emphasized that plateau phase of battery discharge is significant when estimating SOC. This is the most stable period and as such, a good indicator of battery performance. However, when the BTMS is not efficient to dissipate the heat or to keep the temperature, the general performance of the battery may be compromised. As Jaguemont et al. [26] examined the temperature effect on LIBs in low and high temperatures, this factor has an influence on the performance of these batteries, their safety, and their life cycle. Another method that has been discussed in the study is how the internal temperature of batteries can be monitored, both using contact-based and contactless techniques.

These have been widely studied on the thermal characteristics, internal resistance, and degradation characteristics of LIBs during the discharge conditions, but very little has been done when the batteries are being charged. Most of the past studies have placed little significant importance on such phenomena under charging conditions in favor of the discharge conditions in evaluating battery performance, temperature rise, and capacity loss assuming that the behavior is symmetric under charging and discharging conditions. However, the charging process has various different electrochemical and thermal dynamics, including enhanced internal resistance, enhanced polarization effects, and enhanced heat generation relating to entropy, especially at faster C-rates. Such differences can have a major effect on the battery life, thermal management requirement and safety threshold. The primary aim of this research is to experimentally examine the thermal and electrical properties of a battery module under similar environmental conditions and to compare the heat generated during charging and discharging. While previous studies have explored battery behavior during discharge, there is a scarcity of experimental research on the thermal performance of batteries during charging, particularly at the cell level. To address this gap, the current study analyzes a 3S2P battery module at various C-rates during both charging and discharging. Additionally, the study compares heat generation in both processes to offer insights for designing an efficient thermal management for battery modules.

The battery pack comprises of six LG INR21700 M50T Li-ion cells, which have a nominal capacity of 5Ah. These cells will be designed in the form of 3S2P with two cells in parallel to add more capacity and current carrying capability and three parallel modules in series to add more voltage output of the module. This structure is dissimilar to parallel and series connections and offers a combination of the benefits, and it provides a battery module with superior energy density and adequate voltage to address certain requirements of the application. Fig. 1 shows the developed 3S2P configuration of battery module. The battery module is made up of a cylindrical Li-ion battery cell of LG. The key technical specifications and properties of the cell are listed in Table 1. The schematic of the testing setup is illustrated in Fig. 2.

The thermal and electrical performance of a 3S2P battery module with a capacity of 10 Ah and voltage 12 V under varying charge and discharge C-rates is presented in Fig. 3. The experimental setup primarily consists of a 10,000 mAh battery module, a charging and discharging unit with a voltage range (9–99 V), a charging current of 20 A, and a discharging current of 40 A. It also includes a data storage unit and thermocouples for the real-time temperature monitoring. The 3S2P battery configuration was tested at three different C-rates (0.5 C, 0.75 C, and 1 C) under controlled indoor laboratory conditions to simulate operational scenarios without the insulation cover of the cell. To obtain the temperature of each cell throughout the charging and discharging processes, six K-type thermocouples were placed using aluminum foil tape on the surface of the battery. The corresponding temperature read as T1, T2, T3, T4, T5 and T6 were located on the middle surface of the battery cells (can be seen Fig. 1.)

The charging process is carried out using the charging and discharging setup by adjusting current and voltage at different C rates. Thermocouples attached to the individual cells monitor surface temperature during each cycle, and recorded with data acquisition system. The experiment is repeated for each specified C-rate to evaluate the thermal performance. The recorded data is analyzed to assess the effect of different C-rates on cell temperature, identify thermal gradients, and safety of the battery module under varying conditions. The experiments were conducted under indoor laboratory conditions over a period of two days, with the ambient room temperature was around 27°C in the month of January.

To ensure the accuracy and reliability of the experimental data, an uncertainty analysis was performed for the measurement instruments used during the study, such as thermocouples for temperature monitoring and the battery charging/discharging unit for electrical measurements. The temperature measurements were made using calibrated K-type thermocouples with a ± 1.0°C accuracy as specified by the manufacturer. Random errors were minimized by measuring the temperature at several points on the battery surface and averaging them over time and for the electrical parameters, the battery charging/discharge system had a digital control interface with voltage and current measurement accuracy of ± 0.5% of reading. The total uncertainty for multiple instruments is evaluated using the Root Sum Square (RSS) method [28].

For any variable Q calculated from multiple measured values, the combined uncertainty
U
Q
is given by:
(1)
U
Q
=
U
1
2
+
U
1
2
+ … +
U
n
2
Where
U
1
,
U
2
, …
U
n
are the individual uncertainties for each instruments.
Thus, the calculated total uncertainty in temperature, voltage and current are around ±1.02°C, ± 0.06 V and ± 0.05 A, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal performance of battery module during discharging and charging process
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4(a–c) illustrates the temperature variation of individual cells (Tc1–Tc6) in the 3S2P Li-ion battery module, during constant-current discharge at 0.5 C (5 A), 0.75 C (7.5 A), and 1 C (10 A). In all three cases, the temperature of each cell increases continuously during the initial stage of discharge due to internal heat generation. This heat is mainly produced by irreversible Joule heating and reversible entropy heat. As the discharge current increases, the irreversible heat generation increases significantly because it depends on the square of the current. As a result, the temperature rise becomes steeper and the peak temperature becomes higher at higher discharge rates. At 0.5 C discharge (Fig. 4a), the cell temperatures increase gradually, with peak temperatures ranging from 30.1°C (Tc1) to 31.8°C (Tc5) over a discharge duration of 7200 s. At 0.75 C (Fig. 4b), the temperature rise becomes faster and the peak temperature increases to 35.2°C, while the discharge duration reduces to 5400 s. At 1 C (Fig. 4c), the temperature rise is more rapid, and the maximum temperature reaches 38.0°C within 3600 s. This behavior confirms that higher discharge current leads to higher heat generation and faster thermal response.

Moreover, it is found that there is a visible difference in temperature between the cells at any rate of discharge, where Tc5 has the highest temperature, and Tc1 has the lowest temperature. This disparity is largely due to difference in internal resistance, position of cell and heat transfer conditions in the module. Because the amount of heat produced as a result of Joule heating is proportional to the internal resistance, a minute difference in resistance between cells leads to variations in the rate of heat production and trouble temperature. The cells having high internal resistance produce more heat and attain higher temperatures. Moreover, the cells in lower positions with respect to ambient air receive less convection cooling and the resultant saving in heat is added. The higher the discharge rate, the greater the effect of irreversible heat generation, the higher the temperatures and the greater the temperature differences between the cells. The greatest temperature difference between Tc5 and Tc1 at 1 C discharge is 2.7 C, which is more thermal non-uniformity at higher current. The following results indicate that irreversible heat production is the key factor in causing temperature increase and thermal asymmetry, whereas the heat loss in the form of convection and entropy heat influences the overall thermal performance of the battery module.

In addition, a clear temperature difference is observed among the cells at all discharge rates, with Tc5 showing the highest temperature and Tc1 showing the lowest temperature. This difference is mainly caused by variations in internal resistance, cell position, and heat transfer conditions within the module. Since heat generation due to Joule heating depends on internal resistance, even small differences in resistance among cells result in different heat generation rates and temperature rise. Cells with higher internal resistance generate more heat and reach higher temperatures. Furthermore, cells located in positions with lower exposure to ambient air experience reduced convective cooling, which leads to additional heat accumulation. As the discharge rate increases from 0.5 C to 1 C, the effect of irreversible heat generation becomes more dominant, resulting in higher temperatures and larger temperature differences between cells. At 1 C discharge, the maximum temperature difference between Tc5 and Tc₁ is 2.7°C, indicating increased thermal non-uniformity at higher current. These results show that irreversible heat generation is the primary factor responsible for temperature rise and thermal asymmetry, while heat dissipation through convection and entropy heat affects the overall thermal behavior of the battery module.

Fig. 5(a–c) shows the temperature variation of individual cells (Tc1–Tc6) in the 3S2P Li-ion battery module during CC-CV charging at 0.5 C (5 A), 0.75 C (7.5 A), and 1 C (10 A), respectively. In all cases, the temperature of the cells increases steadily during the initial stage of charging due to internal heat generation. This heat is mainly caused by irreversible Joule heating and reversible entropy heat. At 0.5 C charging (Fig. 5a), the temperature increases gradually from about 29°C and reaches a maximum of 38.3°C in Tc5, while Tc1 reaches a lower peak temperature of 36.2°C over the charging duration of 7200 s. When the charging rate increases to 0.75 C (Fig. 5b), the temperature rise becomes faster, and the peak temperature increases further, with Tc₅ reaching 44.1°C and Tc1 reaching 40.3°C within 5400 s. At 1 C charging (Fig. 5c), the temperature rise becomes more significant, and the maximum temperature reaches 48.6°C in Tc5 and 43.2°C in Tc1 within 3600 s. The increased rate of temperature rise at increased charging rates is due to the irreversible generation of heat is proportional to the square of the charging current which leads to increase in the amount of heat within the cells.

A distinct difference in temperature is noted through the charging of the cells with Tc5 having the highest temperature and Tc1 having the lowest temperature at all the charging rates. The reason behind this difference is mainly attributed to the differences in the internal resistance and differences in the heat dissipation conditions in the module. Those cells that have a greater internal resistance produce a lot of heat and thus, will attain higher temperatures. Moreover, the increase of temperature during charging is greater compared to the discharging at the same current rates. Indicatively, the highest temperature in charging is 48.6°C at 1 C and the highest temperature in discharging is 38°C at 1 C. It denotes that when a charge is being charged, the amount of heat generated is greater since there is further polarization and lost on electrochemical consequences. The highest temperature difference between Tc5 and Tc1 during charging is 5.4°C at 1 C and this is larger than the temperature difference between the charging process and discharging. That indicates that charging generates the increased temperatures and temperature change in the battery module. These findings suggest that charging conditions particularly with high current rates are more influential on the temperature increase and thermal non-uniformity which can affect the performance of the battery and stability over time.

The variation of current and voltage behavior for different C rates such as 0.5 C, 0.75 C, and 1 C rates during charging at CC and constant voltage is shown in Fig. 6(a & b). From the Fig. 6(a), it is seen that the when the C-rate is increased, the battery voltage increases at a faster rate within the initial CC mode. In 1 C, the maximum voltage (12.6 V) is achieved within a shorter period of time than that at 0.75 C and 0.5 C, showing an increased rate of electrochemical reaction and rate of Li-ion intercalation at elevated currents. On the other hand, the 0.5 C voltage curve is gradual and takes a very long time to arrive at the cutoff voltage, reflecting a lower rate but more stable charging process. After the cutoff voltage is achieved, all profiles get into the constant voltage phase in which the voltage is constant and the current starts to decline.

This is also evidenced through the plots of current with respect to time, which are shown in Fig. 6(b). From figure it can be seen that the charging current is constant at about 5 A for 0.5 C, 7.5 A for 0.75 C, and 10 A for 1 C during the CC operation, as per the given C-rates. As the battery enters the constant voltage mode, the current starts to drop rapidly, more so for higher C-rates, because the decreased electrochemical driving force as the cell approaches full state-of-charge (SOC). Notably, for 1 C, the current reduction is the quickest, demonstrating a reduced total charging time, while for 0.5 C, the current drop down slowly over a longer period. This contrast illustrates that increased C-rates allow for quicker charging but can result in higher temperature on the cell. On the other hand, decreased C-rates provide a conservative charging profile that can extend cell longevity at the expense of longer charging time.

Fig. 7 shows the voltage-time profiles of a Li-ion battery discharging at CC rates of three different C-rates: 0.5 C, 0.75 C, and 1 C. The curves show the effect of discharge rate on the battery's voltage response. At the beginning of discharge, the three C-rates are close to the same open-circuit voltage (12 V), but they rapidly drift apart as the discharge goes on. The voltage falls more dramatically at higher C-rates, with the 1 C rate having the steepest decline followed by 0.75 C and 0.5 C. This sudden drop at higher C-rates is caused by higher internal resistance and polarization losses, which are more critical under higher currents. As a result, the available capacity (measured in terms of time duration) is much smaller at higher discharge rates. For instance, at 1 C rate, the discharge ends about 1800 s, while at 0.5 C, the battery has a prolonged discharge period running up to about 5800 seconds. Therefore, the voltage profile at 0.5 C rate indicates more efficient use of active material and lower heat generation. Whereas, at 1 C discharge shows an earlier voltage drop, suggesting more rapid depletion of the lithium ions as well as higher over potentials. Moreover, the voltage at the cutoff point (9 V) is achieved much sooner at higher C-rates, demonstrating the balance between power delivery and energy extraction.

Fig. 8 illustrates how the average temperature of the battery module changes during charging and discharging at various C-rates (0.5 C, 0.75 C, and 1 C). It is evident that as the C-rate increases, the module's temperature rises under both conditions. At a 0.5 C discharge rate, the temperature gradually climbs to about 31°C, whereas during charging at the same rate, it reaches nearly 37°C. When the C-rate is elevated to 0.75 C, the temperature increase becomes more pronounced, hitting approximately 34–35°C during discharge and around 41–42°C during charging. At a 1 C rate, the temperature rise is even more significant, with the charging process resulting in the highest temperature of about 45°C, while the discharge temperature remains relatively lower at around 36°C.

These findings suggest that charging generates more heat than discharging at the same current level. Additionally, higher C-rates cause a quicker temperature rise and an earlier peak temperature due to the increased current flowing through the cells. Once the peak temperature is reached, it gradually decreases as heat dissipates into the surrounding environment. Overall, the figure highlights that the thermal behavior of the battery module is heavily influenced by the operating rate, with high-rate charging leading to the most severe thermal conditions.

Fig. 9 illustrates the change of battery module temperature together with voltage variation in the process of discharging at temperature (0.5 C, 0.75 C, and 1 C). It is easily apparent that the thermal response and the behavior of the voltage is very different at higher and higher discharge rate. The temperature increases gradually and the voltage declines gradually at the later part of the discharge period at a moderate rate at 0.5 C. It means that internal heat production is restricted at lower current and the voltage drop is regulated by regular electrochemical processes only. The higher the discharge rate to 0.75 C, the quicker is the increase in temperature and the greater the temperature level at a lower time. Meanwhile, the voltage drops at a faster rate as opposed to 0.5 C. This action is demonstrated by the fact that the greater the current, the more internal resistance loss will take place and hence more heat will be generated within the cells. Increase in temperature also impacts internal resistance which adds to further voltage drop. At 1 C, temperature rises quickly and the maximum rate is observed in all cases, and discharge time is also short and voltage drop is more remarkable. This accelerated rate of reduction of voltage in higher C-rate is primarily explained by the polarization and ohmic losses experienced on high current operation. This figure thus shows that there is a close relation between electrical and thermal behavior of the battery module. Increased rates of discharge not only enhance the rate of internal heat generation but also speed the rate of voltage drop as a result of an increase in internal resistance and polarization. This thermal and electrical stress is increased with the increase in the C-rate therefore it is necessary to control the temperature increment during a high rate operation to ensure consistent performance and avoid the long term degradation.

Fig. 10 illustrates the peak temperatures of the battery module during discharging (Tmax_D) and charging (Tmax_C) at different C-rates (0.5 C, 0.75 C, and 1 C). The results clearly demonstrate that the maximum temperature increases with higher C-rates in both scenarios. During discharging, the temperature peaks rise from 31°C at 0.5 C to 34.5°C at 0.75 C, reaching 36.5°C at 1 C. A similar trend is observed during charging, where the maximum temperature ranges from 37°C at 0.5 C to 42°C at 0.75 C, and reaches 45°C at 1 C. This temperature increase with C-rate is mainly due to the higher current flowing through the cells. Since the irreversible heat generation is proportional to the square of the current (I²R), an increase in C-rate significantly boosts internal heat generation, leading to higher peak temperatures.

Additionally, it is evident that at all C-rates, the peak temperature during charging exceeds that during discharging. The temperature difference between discharging and charging grows with the C-rate, being 6°C at 0.5 C and 8.5°C at 1 C. This indicates that charging produces more heat than discharging at the same current level. The additional heat generation is due to polarization loss and increased electrochemical reactions at higher overpotentials during charging, resulting in greater internal resistance and heat production. The higher the C-rate, the more pronounced these effects become, causing the temperature to rise and the module to experience increased thermal stress. These findings underscore that high-rate charging poses more thermal challenges than discharging, highlighting the need for effective thermal management, especially under fast-charging conditions.

According to Table 2 the temperature gradient of the module increases with an increase in C-rate. At discharging, the variance between the maximum and the minimum average cell temperature rises by 1.33°C at 0.5 C and 2°C at 1 C. Though the increase is moderate, it indicates that increased current magnifies the difference in temperatures between the cells. This is highly exaggerated in the process of charging. The temperature diffuses across the cells at 0.5 C at 1.27°C, and at 1 C at a rapid rate of 4.57°C. This serves to clearly show that higher rates result in more thermal imbalance than discharging. This behavior is caused by the heat generation within the cells that is dependent on the current. The higher the C-rate, the higher the amount of heat that is produced inside each cell. Change in internal resistance, contact resistance or even in cell location causes a visible difference in heat generation. These differences are not much when there is low current, but when there is high current they are amplified and lead to an increase in deviation of temperature.

Besides this, the module will work in the natural convection regime, making it impossible to remove heat. When C-rates are high, the heat production within the cells is greater than the heat loss ability enabling hotter cells to gain more and more heat. This is the reason why some cells will always be registering high temperatures with an increase in rate. The findings verify the temperature non-uniformity increases with C-rate especially when charging is involved, which may lead to non-uniform aging and lower long-term dependability of the battery module.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal management techniques for high C-rates
ce:section.ce:para: According to the experimental findings of this research, the temperature of the battery module rises considerably as the C-rate increases, especially during charging. When charging at higher rates, such as 0.75 C and 1 C, the module's temperature surpasses 40°C, and the temperature disparity between cells becomes more pronounced. These conditions can hasten cell degradation and compromise the battery module's overall reliability. Consequently, implementing an effective thermal management strategy is crucial for high-rate operations.

One viable method is forced air cooling, which involves directing controlled airflow over the battery module to boost convective heat transfer and lower peak temperatures. Another option is incorporating phase change materials (PCM) between adjacent cells. PCM can absorb surplus heat during its phase transition, thus curbing temperature increases and enhancing thermal uniformity. Additionally, altering the cell arrangement by widening the spacing between cells or adding thermally conductive spacers can enhance heat dissipation and minimize localized heat buildup.

To assess the effectiveness of these thermal management strategies, comparative experiments can be performed under the same operating conditions as this study. These experiments might involve testing the baseline module with natural convection, followed by trials with forced air cooling, PCM integration, and adjusted cell spacing. Comparing the maximum temperature and temperature distribution across these configurations will aid in identifying the most effective thermal management solution for high-rate battery operation. Table 3 presents the various thermal management strategies for the battery module when operating at elevated C rates.

The present study explores the experimental investigation of Li-ion battery module of 3S2P configuration having capacity 10Ah, 12.6 V at different C rate for charging and discharging process. The following conclusions are drawn from the study are as follows;
•
The effect of different C-rate during charging and discharging shows the significant impact on temperature of battery module. During charging higher heat is generated in battery module as compared to the discharging. As the C-rate increases the battery module temperature increases.
•
At 1 C, during charging the battery module temperature has reached around 44⁰C maximally, whereas at same C rate, during discharging process achieved a maximum temperature of 35.52⁰C. Also, it is observed that the inner cell experience higher temperature as compared to outer cell due to convection effect and internal resistance of the cell.
•
At 1 C charging rate is recommended for the Li-ion battery module, with an optimal operating temperature range of 15°C to 35°C. However, during charging, significant heat generation occurs, and at a 1 C rate, the temperature exceeds this optimal range, highlighting the need for effective thermal management of the battery module.
•
The higher C-rates reduce charging/discharging time but lead to steeper voltage drops and increased temperature rise, necessitating efficient battery thermal management systems (BTMS) to mitigate thermal load. Whereas the lower C-rates offer better voltage stability and thermal safety, making them more suitable for prolonged battery life in EV applications. The above finding helps the researchers to understand the thermal behavior of battery module to use suitable strategy for battery cooling.

Mangesh Kotambkar: Visualization, Supervision, Project administration. Someshwar Bhakre: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ashwinkumar Dhoble: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), DST, Government of India, for funding this research under the project CRG/2023/003982. We also extend our heartfelt thanks to Visvesvaraya National Institute of Technology (VNIT), Nagpur, for facilitating the experimental work and providing the necessary infrastructure.
